content:
# Ruth Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 880-881 | **Source Image:** RUT_001_page_001.jpg, RUT_001_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass in the days when the judges judged, that there was a famine in the land. And a man went from Bethlehem of Judah to sojourn in the fields of Moab — he and his wife and his two sons.

**2** And the name of the man was Elimelech, and the name of his wife was Naomi, and the names of his two sons were Mahlon and Chilion — Ephrathites from Bethlehem of Judah. And they came to the fields of Moab and remained there.

**3** And Elimelech, the husband of Naomi, died; and she was left — she and her two sons.

**4** And they took for themselves Moabite wives; the name of the one was Orpah, and the name of the second was Ruth. And they dwelt there about ten years.

**5** And both Mahlon and Chilion also died, and the woman was left without her two children and without her husband.

**6** Then she arose, she and her daughters-in-law, and returned from the fields of Moab, for she had heard in the fields of Moab that the LORD had attended to His people by giving them bread.

**7** And she went out from the place where she had been, and her two daughters-in-law were with her. And they went on the way to return to the land of Judah.

**8** And Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, "Go, return, each woman to the house of her mother. May the LORD deal with you in faithful love, as you have dealt with the dead and with me.

**9** May the LORD grant to you that you find rest, each woman in the house of her husband." And she kissed them, and they lifted up their voices and wept.

**10** And they said to her, "No, but we will return with you to your people."

**11** And Naomi said, "Turn back, my daughters. Why would you go with me? Do I yet have sons in my womb that they might become husbands for you?

**12** Turn back, my daughters, go, for I am too old to belong to a husband. Even if I were to say, 'I have hope,' even if I were to belong to a husband tonight, and even if I were to bear sons —

**13** would you wait for them until they were grown? Would you shut yourselves away for them, not belonging to a husband? No, my daughters, for it is far more bitter for me than for you, because the hand of the LORD has gone out against me."

**14** And they lifted up their voices and wept again. And Orpah kissed her mother-in-law, but Ruth clung to her.

**15** And she said, "Behold, your sister-in-law has turned back to her people and to her gods. Turn back after your sister-in-law."

**16** And Ruth said, "Do not press me to abandon you, to turn back from following after you. For where you go, I will go, and where you lodge, I will lodge. Your people shall be my people, and your God shall be my God.

**17** Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. Thus may the LORD do to me, and more besides, if anything but death parts me from you."

**18** And when she saw that she was determined to go with her, she ceased speaking to her.

**19** And the two of them went on until they came to Bethlehem. And it came to pass when they came to Bethlehem that the whole city was stirred on account of them, and the women said, "Is this Naomi?"

**20** And she said to them, "Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.

**21** I went out full, and the LORD has brought me back empty. Why would you call me Naomi, when the LORD has testified against me, and the Almighty has brought calamity upon me?"

**22** So Naomi returned, and Ruth the Moabitess, her daughter-in-law, was with her, who returned from the fields of Moab. And they came to Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **חֶסֶד (hesed), v. 8** — Rendered "faithful love" throughout Ruth, capturing both the covenantal loyalty and affectionate kindness of this term. The LORD's hesed is invoked as a standard of the love Naomi's daughters-in-law have shown.

2. **דָּבַק (davaq), v. 14** — Translated "clung to" for Ruth's refusal to leave Naomi. This is the same verb used of marriage in Genesis 2:24 ("a man shall... cling to his wife"), and its use here signals the depth of Ruth's covenantal commitment.

3. **שְׂדֵי מוֹאָב (sedey Mo'av)** — Rendered "fields of Moab" rather than "country of Moab," following the construct form literally. The Hebrew suggests agricultural territory.

4. **מָרָא (Mara), v. 20** — Naomi's self-renaming: "Naomi" means "pleasant" or "my delight," while "Mara" means "bitter." The wordplay is essential to the narrative.

5. **שַׁדַּי (Shaddai), vv. 20-21** — Rendered "the Almighty," the traditional formal equivalent. Naomi invokes this divine name in her lament, emphasizing God's sovereign power over her suffering.

6. **Verse structure** — Hebrew narrative convention opens nearly every verse with waw-consecutive (וַיְהִי, וַתֹּאמֶר, etc.). These are retained as "And..." to preserve the sequential flow of the Hebrew storytelling.

— LOGOS
